- Mustard highlight keywords: "40,000", "95% FRANCHISED", "5-6% RENT", "4-6% ROYALTY", "RAY KROC", "REAL ESTATE"
- Suppress captions at beats where impact text is already carrying the moment: 0:02–0:05 (hook impact text), 0:46–0:50 (Kroc card), 1:15–1:20 (payoff — silence).

**Lane signature label:** "REAL BUSINESS MODEL" (Poppins ExtraBold, 60pt, mustard, 60% opacity, bottom-left corner) at 0:15 (Reveal beat, when "Rent + Royalty" mechanism begins). Use only if the frame is clean and not already crowded with percentage bars. Omit if the on-screen chart is visually busy — the mechanism itself signals the Smart Money lane.

## 5. Audio (music + SFX + master)

- **Music:** Lo-fi ambient (e.g., YT Audio Library "Calm Piano," Pixabay "Thinking Music," or Epidemic Sound "Focus" playlist) · -22dB throughout · -25dB at quiet beat (1:10–1:15) · entrance at 0:00 (fade in over first 1–2s) · exit after payoff (fade out 1:20–1:25).

- **SFX (3 total, minimal):**

| # | Time | Sound | Volume |
|---|------|-------|--------|
| 1 | 0:06.5 | Light door unlock / open (single clean tone) — franchisee unlocking restaurant | -20dB (subtle) |
| 2 | 0:09 / 0:10 | Soft "ping" or location-marker light (on world map animation) | -22dB (background) |
| 3 | 0:19 | Page turn or paper shuffle (franchisee signing contract) | -20dB (subtle) |

- **Master:** -14 LUFS integrated, peak ≤ -1 dBFS · voice HPF 80Hz, light 200–300Hz cut if muddy, +2dB 3–5kHz max, light de-ess 5–7kHz, 3:1 compression ratio, no reverb / pitch correction / doubling. Deliver as stereo WAV, final MP4 at 128 kbps AAC.

## 6. Color & Look

- **Palette:** navy #162447 / mustard #E8B04B / white #FFFFFF · no red or secondary colors unless framing a current-affairs claim (not applicable here).
- **B-roll grade:** saturation –5 to 0 (slightly muted, not vibrant), temperature +2 to +5K (warm, corporate-friendly), vignette 20–25%, contrast +5.
- **Treatment:** Clean, professional, measured. No sepia, no heavy grading. Numbers and text must read instantly. Graphics appear one at a time, no stacked overlays.
- **Graphics:** Large, bold numbers (percentages, dollar figures); plain white or mustard text on navy; single sans-serif font (Poppins for impact, Inter for body). No decorative scripts, 3D, glows, shadows, or animations beyond simple fade/slide.

## 7. Thumbnails (3 concepts)

### Concept A — Text-Dominant
- **Text:** MCDONALD'S RENTS MORE THAN IT SELLS
- **Composition / palette / subject:** Navy background with large mustard "REAL BUSINESS" label (top-left), white text center. No image; text only. Simple, direct.
- **Why it works:** The headline drives home the counterintuitive claim immediately; viewer recognizes the payoff before clicking. Smart Money aesthetic (text authority, no hype imagery).

### Concept B — Number + Brand
- **Text:** $10.4B RENT (large, 100pt mustard) / "Not Burgers" (smaller, 60pt white, sub-text)
- **Composition / palette / subject:** Navy background; McDonald's golden arches or simplified storefront silhouette in faded white (bottom-right, 30% opacity); number is the focal point.
- **Why it works:** The number ($10.4B) is the hook; the arches signal McDonald's without trademark risk (silhouette/generic). Viewer knows the topic immediately.

### Concept C — Metaphor
- **Text:** THE BURGER IS THE COVER (large, mustard) / THE LEASE IS THE BUSINESS (smaller, white)
- **Composition / palette / subject:** Navy background; split-frame: left = simple burger icon (white, 20% opacity), right = key/lock icon (mustard, 20% opacity). Text bridges both.
- **Why it works:** Payoff lands as the hook; visually cryptic enough to spark curiosity ("what does that mean?"), while Smart Money tone (no emoji, clean icons) keeps it restrained.

**Recommended:** Concept B (Number + Brand). The $10.4B figure is the script's strongest proof line; pairing it with the arches creates instant recognition and credibility. Concept A is solid secondary if the arches feel cluttered. Avoid Concept C in first release (too oblique for cold discovery); use it as a retest variant after initial performance.

## 8. Must-Keep Visual References & Licensing

- **Show real screenshot/footage at:** None required. Script does not reference a specific SEC filing, news article, or copyrighted source that needs visible proof.

- **Recreate as clean text card:** "$10.4B rent figure" (already handled as impact text at 0:02) · "Rent + Royalty percentages" (already stacked bar at 0:25–0:35) · "Ray Kroc insight" (archival photo at 0:42–0:50 OR stylized in-house graphic).

- **Do not use / do not screenshot:** 
  - Live McDonald's storefront video (trademark risk) — use generic "For Lease on commercial storefront" stock instead.
  - Specific SEC 10-K document pages (proprietary, clutter) — reference in video description only.
  - Real franchisee's face or personal identifying detail — use generic franchise operator footage (no names, no close-ups of individuals).

- **Attribution / licensing required:** 
  - Archival Ray Kroc photo (if used) → credit in video description: "Archival image courtesy of McDonald's Corporation history archives."
  - Music track → credit per license (YT Audio Library automatic; Pixabay = "Music by [Artist]"; Epidemic = in-app).
  - Stock B-roll platform → standard credit per their terms (most waive on-screen credits for platforms like Envato, Pexels, Unsplash).

- **Editor decisions & cautions:** 
  - **For Lease sign sourcing:** The script emphasizes McDonald's locations specifically, but using trademarked storefront footage risks copyright claim. Solution: Source a generic commercial "For Lease" sign on a storefront (use Unsplash, Pexels, or Envato "retail storefront" clips), or build a clean in-house navy/white card reading "FOR LEASE" to imply the concept without showing actual McDonald's branding.
  - **Global map:** Simple animated pin-light on a world map is stronger and faster to produce than elaborate 3D spinning globes. Keep the animation to 3–4s, then cut away.
  - **Quiet beat placement:** Do not move off-host or introduce new visuals at 1:10–1:15; retention requires stillness and simplicity.
  - **Fallback for percentages:** If in-house animation is unavailable, use a clean static stacked bar image with text labels; it will land just as effectively.
  - **Deliverable caution:** The script includes specific percentages (5-6% rent, 4-6% royalty) that were verified against SEC 10-K in Stage 3. Ensure these numbers remain visible and legible on-screen; do not simplify to "5%" or "6%" if the original was "5-6%" (the range is the factual claim).

## 9. References & Sources

| Source title | URL | Claim it supports |
|---|---|---|
| McDonald's Corporation 2025 Form 10-K, SEC | https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/63908/000006390826000035/mcd-20251231.htm | McDonald's made $10.4 billion from rent in 2025; 95% of restaurants are franchised; McDonald's owns or controls land beneath franchised locations. |
| Investopedia: "How McDonald's Really Makes Money" | https://www.investopedia.com/articles/markets/032015/how-mcdonalds-makes-its-money-mcd.asp | Ray Kroc founded the franchise model in the 1950s; real estate strategy is central to corporate profitability. |

title: Why Food Delivery Apps Never Killed the Mumbai Dabbawala

- **Title:** Why Food Delivery Apps Never Killed the Mumbai Dabbawala
- **Hook stack:**
  Swiggy and Zomato can deliver anything to your door in twenty minutes.
  Anything except a tiffin your mother made at home.
  That is why five thousand men are still moving lunchboxes across Mumbai by train.
- **Payoff:** Apps compete with restaurants. The dabbawala competes with your kitchen — different war entirely.
- **Host tone:** Energy 5/10, Pace measured with slight quickening through anaphora; slow on "An empty office." and "Exactly."; full stop after "Different war entirely.", Warmth dry-irony; half-smile on "Anything except a tiffin your mother made at home"
- **Strong video title:** Why Food Delivery Apps Never Killed the Mumbai Dabbawala
- **Lane signature label:** "REAL REASON →" at pivot line ("But apps and dabbawalas were never selling the same thing.") — displayed only, never spoken

**Locked spoken script (verbatim):**

> Swiggy and Zomato can deliver anything to your door in twenty minutes.
> Anything except a tiffin your mother made at home.
> That is why five thousand men are still moving lunchboxes across Mumbai by train.
> Swiggy had GPS tracking.
> The dabbawala did not.
> Swiggy had thousands of restaurants.
> The dabbawala had one meal.
> Swiggy had real-time updates.
> The dabbawala had a hand-coded marking on a tin box.
> This system has been running since 1890.
> Every condition for disruption was there.
> But apps and dabbawalas were never selling the same thing.
> A Swiggy order comes from a restaurant kitchen.
> A dabbawala picks up the tiffin from the worker's home every morning.
> It crosses Mumbai on the local train network.
> It reaches the office at lunch.
> Home-cooked food, on rails.
> That is a product no delivery app ever offered.
> At roughly one to two thousand rupees a month, it costs far less than daily restaurant orders.
> So the apps arrived.
> And the dabbawala carried on.
> Right?
> But the system that survived Swiggy and Zomato has a different problem.
> An empty office.
> When office workers stopped commuting regularly, the routes disappeared.
> The customer base reportedly fell by more than half since the pandemic.
> Not because an app competed.
> Because the office stopped existing.
> Exactly.
> Apps compete with restaurants.
> The dabbawala competes with your kitchen.
> Different war entirely.
> The system nobody could automate is being dissolved by the commute that never came back

### Concept B — Subject + Text

- **Text:** APPS LOST THE WRONG WAR

"APPS LOST THE WRONG WAR" in Poppins ExtraBold white (right 55%), mustard on "WRONG". Navy background behind text. The tin is the unfamiliar object that creates visual curiosity.
- **Why it works:** The coded tin is visually distinctive and Indian-specific — the viewer has never seen it, which creates a stop-and-look moment. The text resolves the curiosity gap.

### Concept C — Number-Dominant

- **Text:** 5,000 / STILL ON THE RAILS
- **Composition / palette / subject:** "5,000" in Poppins ExtraBold mustard, 50% frame height, centered. "STILL ON THE RAILS" in Inter Bold white below, significantly smaller. Navy background. No imagery.
- **Why it works:** The number is surprising in the context of an app-dominated city. "Still on the rails" is literal (trains) and figurative (surviving). Creates a mismatch curiosity gap between the number and the viewer's assumption.

**Recommended:** A — "DIFFERENT WAR ENTIRELY" is the script's own payoff line, already selected as thumbnail text in the locked script. It passes the channel's named-pattern test and is the line most likely to be repeated. Must pass: 3 words, brand palette, two approved fonts, no banned visuals or words, completes rather than duplicates the title.

  - Any social media posts or personal user profiles referencing dabbawalas
  - Low-resolution tourist photos of dabbawalas — they exist widely online but are often personally identifiable and improperly licensed

- **Attribution / licensing required:**
  - Archival Bombay / 1890 footage or stills: confirm public domain or Creative Commons license from Wikimedia Commons before use; credit in video description if CC-BY required
  - Dabbawala documentary footage (Hook 3, mechanism beat): if sourced from news agencies or documentaries, verify license; Wikimedia Commons and archive.org have free-to-use footage — use those where possible; credit in description
  - Stock footage (delivery bike, tiffin, empty office, WFH): confirm royalty-free commercial license on Pexels, Pixabay, Coverr, or Mixkit before use

- **Editor decisions & cautions:** The split-screen anaphora at 0:06–0:14 requires the editor to hold two visual elements simultaneously — the in-house Swiggy text card on left and the dabbawala tin close-up on right. This is the one frame that carries two elements; keep both elements minimal (no more than one text label per side) so it does not violate the single-element restraint principle. The payoff three-beat sequence at 0:52–0:58 uses text cards rather than host face — this is intentional; do not switch back to host mid-sequence. The identity line close can be either host face or clean text card depending on the host's delivery energy at that line; use host if the delivery is flat and settled, text card if there is any residual animation in the frame.

title: What Actually Explains Maruti's 40 Percent Market Share
script:
# Editor Brief: Maruti Ownership Moat
*Smart Money / Smart Business · Anchor Short · 90–120s · Treatment register: Default (analytical)*

---

## 1. Script (locked — do not change a word)

- **Title:** What Actually Explains Maruti's 40 Percent Market Share
- **Hook stack:**
  Maruti Suzuki has 40 percent of India's car market.
  Price is the answer everyone gives.
  But in 2024, Maruti also held 35.9 percent of India's used-car market — and price does not explain that.
- **Payoff:** Maruti does not sell cheap cars. It sells the lowest-risk ownership bet in India.
- **Host tone:** Energy 4/10, Pace measured → dry observation, Warmth dry-irony
- **Strong video title:** What Actually Explains Maruti's 40 Percent Market Share
- **Lane signature label:** SMART MONEY → at the pivot beat (0:23–0:32), small chip lower-left. Not used if the frame is already carrying a number — place only during the pivot lines when no number is on screen.

**Locked spoken script (verbatim):**

> Maruti Suzuki has 40 percent of India's car market.
> Price is the answer everyone gives.
> But in 2024, Maruti also held 35.9 percent of India's used-car market — and price does not explain that.
>
> The Alto is one of the cheapest passenger cars in India. True.
> The WagonR is among India's most-sold cars. True.
> Hyundai makes affordable cars.
> Tata makes affordable cars.
> None of them come close to Maruti's share in the used-car market.
>
> Because used-car dominance is not about what the car costs when you buy it.
> It is about what the car is worth when someone else wants to buy it from you.
>
> First: the service network.
> Maruti runs more than 5,900 service workshops.
> They span 3,000 Indian cities.
> Not showrooms.
> Workshops.
> In Aurangabad. In Madurai. In Haridwar.
> Every roadside mechanic in this country knows how to fix a Maruti.
> Parts are available at almost every auto-parts shop in the country.
> A Maruti breaks down — someone nearby can fix it, today.
> In a smaller city with most other brands, you wait for parts to arrive.
>
> Second: the resale.
> Maruti's 35.9 percent used-car market share means buyers are always there.
> Which means when you sell, you get a real price.
> Which means your cost of ownership over five years is lower than the sticker suggests.
>
> The price gets you into the showroom.
> What keeps Maruti dominant is everything after the showroom.
> The service network is the product.
> The resale floor is the product.
> The peace of mind on a midnight highway is the product.
>
> Maruti does not sell cheap cars. It sells the lowest-risk ownership bet in India.

### Concept A — Number-Dominant (Layout Type 3)

### Concept B — Text-Dominant (Layout Type 1)
- **Text:** "OWNERSHIP NOT PRICE" (3 words)

### Concept C — Text-Dominant, mental model variant (Layout Type 1)
- **Text:** "LOWEST RISK WINS" (3 words)
- **Composition:** Full-frame navy. "LOWEST RISK" in white Poppins ExtraBold. "WINS" in mustard, dropped slightly below. No imagery.
- **Why it works:** Names the transferable principle — appeals to a viewer who sees business/money content broadly, not just car buyers. Completes the title rather than duplicating it.

**Recommended:** Concept A — the 35.9% number is surprising enough to stop the scroll, and the "NOT PRICE" sub-text creates the curiosity gap without explanation. Tests well at small size because the number dominates even at thumbnail dimensions.

All three pass: ≤5 words, brand palette only (navy/mustard/white), Poppins ExtraBold + Inter Bold, no banned visuals, no banned words, no emoji, completes not duplicates the title.

title: India Doesn't Have One Monsoon. It Has Two.
script:
# Editor Brief: India Does Not Have One Monsoon Season. It Has Two.
*Hidden India · Anchor Short · ~108s · Treatment register: Science*

---

## 1. Script (locked — do not change a word)

- **Title:** India Doesn't Have One Monsoon. It Has Two.
- **Hook stack:**
  When Mumbai's monsoon ends in September, Chennai's monsoon is just beginning.
  Same country.
  Different ocean.
- **Payoff:** India does not have a monsoon. It has two — from different oceans, months apart.
- **Host tone:** Energy 5/10, Pace: measured (deliberate pauses after "Chennai." and "Different ocean."), Warmth: dry observation — quiet revelation, not announcement.
- **Strong video title:** India Doesn't Have One Monsoon. It Has Two.
- **Lane signature label:** "HIDDEN INDIA" — small label, bottom-right corner, at SW Monsoon reveal beat (~0:40). Frame carries the map visual; label does not crowd it.

**Locked spoken script (verbatim):**

> When Mumbai's monsoon ends in September, Chennai's monsoon is just beginning.
> Same country.
> Different ocean.
>
> Most of India learns one monsoon.
> It starts in Kerala, in June.
> Moves north.
> Reaches Delhi by July.
> India's farms, India's dams, India's reservoirs.
> All timed to this one system.
> There is just one thing this picture leaves out.
> Chennai.
>
> Chennai does not get much rain in June or July.
> In the weeks that Mumbai is flooding, Chennai is dry.
> This is not a delay.
> Chennai's rains come from a completely different system.
>
> The monsoon most people know is called the Southwest Monsoon.
> It starts in June.
> It comes from the Arabian Sea.
> It accounts for roughly three-quarters of India's annual rainfall.
> It is large. It is consistent. It is what most of India runs on.
>
> But in October, something changes.
> The winds reverse direction.
> The Bay of Bengal takes over.
> A second monsoon arrives from the northeast.
> October. November. December.
> Different ocean. Different wind direction. Different arrival.
>
> For Tamil Nadu, this second system brings more rain than the first one does.
> Not the June rains that fill Mumbai's lakes and Delhi's rivers.
> A Bay of Bengal system that arrives four months later, from the opposite direction.
>
> A farmer in Kerala plants in June.
> A farmer in Tamil Nadu plants in October.
> Same country.
> Different farming year.
>
> India does not have a monsoon.
> It has two.
> And they have never arrived together.
>
> Every Indian has lived through both.
> The second one just never got the headline.

---

## 2. Shot List (scene by scene)

## 3. B-Roll Placement Guide

| Consequence — Tamil Nadu paddy | 1:15–1:22 | Tamil Nadu paddy field or river delta scene — crop in mid-growth or harvesting, October-November look | B-roll | Editor's choice — stock. Tamil Nadu agricultural landscape. |

**Stay on host face:** Pre-reveal beat (0:30–0:40), Farmer parallel (1:22–1:28), Quiet beat (1:28–1:30), Payoff (1:31–1:43 — host face under faded map).

**Must be real / archival only:** None — no beat in this script requires authentic archival footage as proof. All B-roll is illustrative. The meteorological claims are supported by IMD data cited in the description, not by showing specific screenshots.

**In-house text cards (build these):**
1. Impact text: "SAME COUNTRY." — white, Poppins ExtraBold, ~0:00–0:03
2. Impact text: "DIFFERENT OCEAN." — mustard, Poppins ExtraBold, ~0:03–0:07
3. Impact text: "CHENNAI." — white, Poppins ExtraBold, ~0:22–0:24
4. Map label: "Arabian Sea" — white, Inter Bold, on map at ~0:40

**Map animation build notes:** India map outline on navy background. Two distinct sweeps:
- White sweep = Southwest Monsoon. Enters from lower-left (Arabian Sea). Moves up the west coast, fans northeast across the interior. Labels: "Arabian Sea" / "June–September."
- Mustard sweep = Northeast Monsoon. Enters from upper-right (Bay of Bengal). Hits eastern coast and Tamil Nadu. Labels: "Bay of Bengal" / "October–December."

---

## 4. On-Screen Text

**Impact text (Poppins ExtraBold — 3 uses total):**

title: Samsung's First Product Was Not a Smartphone
script:
## 1. Script (locked — do not change a word)
- **Title:** Samsung's First Product Was Not a Smartphone
- **Hook stack:**
  Samsung's first product was not a smartphone.
  It was not electronics of any kind.
  Samsung sold dried fish, vegetables, and noodles.
- **Payoff:** Samsung did not choose excellence. The government removed the alternative.
- **Host tone:** Energy 6/10, Pace measured, Warmth dry observation
- **Strong video title:** Samsung's First Product Was Not a Smartphone
- **Lane signature label:** "SMART BUSINESS ->" — small text, bottom-left corner, at the first government-condition line. Never spoken.

**Locked spoken script (verbatim):**

> Samsung did not start by building phones.
>
> It started by selling dried fish.
>
> In 1938, Lee Byung-chul opened a small trading firm in Daegu. It moved fish, vegetables, noodles, and groceries from Korea to nearby markets.
>
> For almost thirty years, this was Samsung: not a lab, not a factory, not a technology company.
>
> The change came because South Korea made a new kind of business impossible to ignore.
>
> In the early 1960s, the country's military government began tying corporate survival to exports. Companies that earned foreign currency received subsidised loans, import permits, foreign-exchange access, and state backing. Companies that missed targets could lose the support that made growth possible.
>
> This was not a clean success story. It happened under an authoritarian government, inside a political system nobody should romanticise. But the pressure it created was real.
>
> Samsung was still a trading company when that pressure started reshaping the economy. Selling groceries could build a business in Korea. It could not build one that survived in an export-driven state.
>
> So Samsung moved.
>
> First into sugar, textiles, insurance, and retail. Then into heavier industry. Then, in 1969, into electronics.
>
> The company that once sold noodles began making black-and-white televisions for export.
>
> Not because Lee Byung-chul had seen the smartphone age coming.
>
> Not because Samsung had always been a technology company waiting to emerge.
>
> Because South Korea had made low-value trading a shrinking place to stand.
>
> The memory chips came later. The global brand came later. The phones came much later.
>
> The first transformation was less glamorous: move into products the world would buy, or watch capital, licences, and state support move to someone else.
>
> That is the part most business stories miss. Companies do not always transform because ambition expands. Sometimes they transform because the old business stops being safe.
>
> Samsung began as a grocery trader.
>
> The future arrived when staying one became the risk.

---

title: One Man Planted a Forest. He Did Not Start With Trees.
script:
## 1. Script (locked — do not change a word)
- **Title:** One Man Planted a Forest. He Did Not Start With Trees.
- **Hook stack:**
  A sandbar in Assam had zero trees in 1979.
  Today it is a 1,360-acre forest with Bengal tigers and over a hundred elephants.
  One man started it. He did not start with trees.
- **Payoff:** You cannot plant a forest. You plant the first link — and the ecosystem assembles the rest.
- **Host tone:** Energy 3/10, Pace: measured (deliberate on bamboo mechanism; faster through animal list), Warmth: restrained
- **Strong video title:** One Man Planted a Forest. He Did Not Start With Trees.
- **Lane signature label:** SCIENCE LITE → at bamboo mechanism beat (0:21) — small, low frame load; omit if the bamboo animation is already carrying the frame.

**Locked spoken script (verbatim):**

> A sandbar in Assam had almost no tree cover in 1979.
>
> Today it is a 1,360-acre forest with Bengal tigers and an elephant herd that visits every year.
>
> One man started it.
>
> But the first move was not planting trees.
>
> In 1979, a sixteen-year-old Mising tribesman named Jadav Payeng walked onto a Brahmaputra river island after a flood.
>
> He found snakes dying on the exposed sand.
>
> No shade. No moisture. Nothing to hold the land together.
>
> Most versions tell this as a tree-planting story.
>
> The first move was soil engineering.
>
> Payeng started with bamboo.
>
> Bamboo does not need established forest soil.
>
> Its roots grip loose sand.
>
> They trap sediment.
>
> They pull organic matter into the ground.
>
> Slowly, the sandbar starts behaving less like sand and more like soil.
>
> Moisture stays.
>
> Shade appears.
>
> Ground temperature drops.
>
> Then other plants can survive — grasses, shrubs, seedlings — where nothing had held before.
>
> And then the animals came.
>
> Not because anyone placed them there.
>
> Because the conditions now existed.
>
> Deer came first.
>
> Then predators.
>
> Then elephants. A herd of around a hundred now visits the forest every year.
>
> Some years, calves are born inside it.
>
> In 2008, forest officials followed an elephant herd into the area.
>
> That was when they realised the sandbar had become a forest.
>
> Nearly thirty years of one man planting bamboo, and bamboo doing what bamboo does to sand.
>
> The forest is now named Molai, after Jadav.
>
> It holds tigers, deer, birds, and elephants in a place that began as exposed river sand.
>
> You do not plant a forest.
>
> You plant the first link.
>
> And when the conditions are right, the ecosystem assembles the rest.

title: Snakes Don't Attack You. They're Trying to Escape.

## 1. Script (locked — do not change a word)
- **Title:** Snakes Don't Attack You. They're Trying to Escape.
- **Hook stack:**
  Fifty-eight thousand Indians die from snakebites every year.
  The snake that killed them was not hunting them.
  It was trying to leave.
- **Payoff:** The snake does not want you. It wants the exit you are blocking.
- **Host tone:** Energy 5/10, Pace: measured (slightly faster through prey list; pause after "You were never on the list." and after "And withholds."; slowest at payoff), Warmth: half-smile — matter-of-fact about something surprising
- **Strong video title:** Snakes Don't Attack You. They're Trying to Escape.
- **Lane signature label:** `SCIENCE LITE →` — small lower-third at Reveal 1 entry (0:15), on-screen only, never spoken

**Locked spoken script (verbatim):**

> India loses an estimated fifty-eight thousand people to snakebites every year.
>
> Most of those snakes were not hunting humans.
> They were trying to survive them.
>
> We tell one story about snakes: predators that coil, strike, inject venom, and appear where people live and work. So the encounter feels intentional.
>
> But predators bite what they can eat.
>
> The snakes behind most medically serious bites in India — cobra, krait, Russell’s viper, and saw-scaled viper — live in a smaller world: rodents, frogs, lizards, birds, and animals close enough to swallow.
>
> A human is not prey.
> A human is a threat.
>
> That difference matters because venom is not random. A snake uses it to immobilise food or survive danger. It can vary how much it delivers, and sometimes delivers none at all.
>
> So why do people still die?
>
> Because the encounter happens at the wrong distance.
>
> A snake reads vibration through the ground. It reads chemicals through its tongue. It feels a heavy animal arrive before it fully knows what that animal is.
>
> In India, this often happens in fields.
>
> A farmer steps near grass, crop cover, or a bund. The snake is there. The human is too close. The exit is blocked. The strike is not a planned attack.
>
> It is panic with fangs.
>
> This does not make snakes safe. A bite is always a medical emergency.
>
> But it changes the story.
>
> Snakebite deaths are not caused by snakes seeking humans. They are caused by two species meeting at the exact distance where one cannot escape and the other cannot react in time.
>
> The snake does not want you.
>
> It wants the one thing you accidentally took away.
>
> A way out.
